texts.full_text: walked, the familiar Christmas carol, and it had guided him to this place. The forest, usually a maze of snow-laden trees, had seemed to welcome him, the path clear and easy to follow. The snow had whispered as he walked, the only sound, a soft, almost comforting, noise. The silence had been a welcome companion, and he had almost felt as if he had been expected. As he entered, he felt a sense of peace and a welcome warmth. The fire, a comforting glow, had been a sight for sore eyes, and he had felt a sense of coming home. The cottage, a place of safety, had been a haven. He had expected a greeting, a welcoming embrace, and he had not been disappointed. The feeling of warmth and safety had been a comforting blanket, and he had felt a sense of relief. He knew there was someone waiting for him. The door creaked as he closed it, the force of his hand causing some of the snow that had gathered above to fall. He touched his gloves to his reddened, cold cheeks as he faced the inside of the cottage.

To conduct a preliminary investigation of the rpotential of the MacHANd, a novel assessment tool, we conducted a pilot study. The MacHANd, which stands for Motor Assessment of the Hand and Arm in Neurotrauma, is designed to evaluate the functional capabilities of individuals with traumatic hand injuries. The pilot study involved a small group of participants, all of whom had sustained traumatic hand injuries. The MacHANd was administered to these participants to assess their hand function across various tasks. texts.full_text: nce of national economic growth upon water should 89 Some Economic Considerations Involved in Water Pollution Control G. E. McCallum, Chief Water Supply and Water Pollution Control Program Public Health Service U. S. Department of Health, Education, & Welfare Washington, D. C. The importance of clean water has been recognized since ancient times. Evidence of this recognition is seen in the comment of the Greek tragic poet Aeschylus who said: "But if with streams defiled and tainted soil, Clear river thou pollute, no drink thou'lt find!" This observation is as it was in the Fifth Century, B. C. Its portent for the future is ominous. Since the beginning of World War II, this country has become a tremendous water user and is suffering many of the "growing pains" associated with rapid development. We are experiencing increasing competition for available waters and sharper conflicts between water users. Also, we are all aware that water use for all purposes is increasing rapidly. We are, therefore, confronted not only with today's water resources problems, but with planning for tomorrow's needs as well. In the years ahead, an important share of the nation's welfare will depend on our ability to accommodate all water uses in a reasonable and equitable manner. texts.full_text: r Facebook accountClose EDITORIAL: Anti-business bills threaten good jobs No Colorado legislative session would be complete without a spate of "messaging bills" designed to regulate private businesses and burden them with new expenses and procedures. As explained in this space April 12, House Bill 1305 would establish a "ban the box" law, prohibiting employers from asking about criminal history on initial job applications. While the intent is laudable—giving those with past convictions a fairer chance at employment—the implementation could burden businesses with additional compliance costs and paperwork. Moreover, Senate Bill 206 aims to require all Colorado companies that do business with state or local government agencies to pay their employees no less than $15 per hour. Again, the goal of lifting workers out of poverty is commendable; however, such a mandate can strain small businesses operating on thin margins and may lead to reduced hiring or increased automation. Another bill, House Bill 1234, proposes new regulations for gig economy companies like Uber and Lyft by reclassifying their independent contractors as employees. This could significantly increase the operational costs of these platforms due to payroll taxes, workers' compensation insurance, and other employee benefits—a change that might not only stifle innovation but also reduce flexibility for those who rely on such work. These bills may carry good intentions, yet they can collectively create a regulatory environment where businesses—especially small ones—are discouraged from expanding or hiring. In the long term, this could harm economic growth in Colorado and limit job opportunities precisely when we should be fostering an atmosphere that encourages business development and entrepreneurship. The legislature must strike a balance between protecting workers' rights and ensuring a viable climate for all types of enterprises to thrive. texts.full_text: skip to sidebar The New Hartman home posts rss comments rss edit It's always something, right!?! Posted by Lisa on Monday, December 12, 2011 Life can be complicated at times, but when there's a baby involved it seems like everything becomes just that much more complicated. There are new factors to consider, the biggest being that you can't leave a a baby. Ever. Take this morning for example. DH was all ready to leave this morning. He goes down to the garage, gets in his car and tries to start the engine, but it won't budge. He gets out, checks the battery, and realizes it's dead. Now, normally this wouldn't be a huge issue, but with a baby in the picture, it's a whole different story. He can't just leave the baby with me and go jump start the car, because I have to get ready for the day too, and the baby needs to be fed and changed. So, we have to figure out a whole new plan, and fast, because we can't be late for our appointments. It's moments like these that I'm reminded of just how much our lives have changed since the baby arrived

texts.full_text: parental rights. Factual Background Appellant is the father of two minor children, John Doe (born April 2010) and Jane Doe (born June 2012). The Arkansas Department of Human Services (DHS) took custody of the children in December 2013 after allegations of abuse and neglect. DHS developed a case plan for Appellant, which included requirements such as attending parenting classes, submitting to random drug tests, and obtaining stable employment and housing. Trial Court Proceedings At the termination hearing, evidence was presented showing that Appellant had failed to comply with the case plan requirements despite being given ample opportunities to do so. The DHS social worker testified that Appellant repeatedly missed scheduled visitations, tested positive for illicit substances on multiple occasions, and did not secure stable employment or housing. The trial court found that there was clear and convincing evidence that Appellant had failed to remedy the conditions leading to the removal of his children and that termination of parental rights was in the best interest of the children. The court issued an order terminating Appellant's parental rights. Issue on Appeal Appellant contends that the trial court erred in finding clear and convincing evidence to support the termination of his parental rights, asserting that he made reasonable efforts to comply with the case plan but was hindered by circumstances beyond his control. Standard of Review The standard for reviewing a termination of parental rights is whether there is clear and convincing evidence to support the trial court’s findings. This Court will affirm the decision if the evidence supports it, even in light of evidence to the contrary. See , 2015 Ark. App. 478. Analysis In order for parental rights to be terminated, the State must demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that: (1) grounds exist under Ark. Code Ann. § 9-27-341; and (2) termination is in the best interest of the child(ren). See , 2014 Ark. App. 589. The relevant portion of Ark. texts.full_text: uidance. These prayers, in the Muslim world, are just as much for self-improvement as they are for the benefit of God—each time our fictional Muslim prays, he is reminded of God’s glory, of His laws, and of His love. At some point in the day, it is likely that our Muslim will give to his favorite charity, or at least make plans to do so. The Quran—the Muslim holy text—uplifts the act of giving to the poor as a worthy endeavor, and so acts of giving are at the forefront of Muslim thought. When the afternoon arrives, he is as ready to go home as the rest of us, but before he leaves, he performs the Asr prayer, in which he takes the time to reflect on God and the greater meaning of life. The social scene he engages in after work can look quite different than the usual American’s if romance is involved. Traditionally, Muslims do not date in the western sense, and so our fictional fellow may face challenges here in the West. If he does spend time with a member of the opposite sex, it may be in a group outing, amongst friends or family. One-on-one dates will be exceedingly rare. Dating aside, he will spend time with his male friends just as anyone else would, albeit without the use of alcohol, which is forbidden in Islam. texts.full_text: evin Casey Tags: CVE • Radicalization • Transition • Research A review of “Conflict, Violent Extremism and Development: New Challenges, New Responses” One of the most acute challenges of working in conflict zones is differentiating between “legitimate” conflict actors and extremist groups. In countries such as Libya and Syria, state collapse has led to a proliferation of armed groups and institutions—all claiming legitimacy and exerting authority over populations. Among these are groups that most observers consider “extreme” in that they are internationally recognized as groups or they use terrorist tactics to coerce populations. READ MORE Engaging Women in PVE Programming By Megan Bromberg Tags: CVE • Gender • Radicalization Recent research by DAI’s Enhancing Governance, Accountability, and Engagement (ENGAGE) project team in Mindanao demonstrates that while membership in violent extremist groups is overwhelmingly male, there is limited evidence to suggest that support for violent extremist ideologies differs fundamentally between the sexes. READ MORE What To Do About Fake News? Ask Kenya By Skye Perske Tags: #FakeNews • Governance • Transition So-called news is an issue du jour in Washington and beyond. While it has undoubtedly been hyped for reasons, fake news is real, its dangers clear and present—especially in environments. Even in the United States, the Pizzagate conspiracy theory and subsequent Comet Ping Pong shooting in Washington, D.C., in December 2016 shows how quickly fake news can turn to real violence. Internationally, journalist Stephanie Busari has shown how fake news allows us to be passive in dismissing real atrocities as “hoaxes,” highlighting how Nigerian authorities’ use of the “fake news” led to loss of life and increased suffering for the Chibok girls of northern Nigeria at the hands of Boko Haram. No. 4,649,116) proposes a method by which micro-size magnetic particles are separated from a liquid by using a magnetic rod which is pushed thereto. The particles are pulled off the rod by using a stronger magnet. Publication WO-86/06493 suggests a method to be used in immunoassays, in which an open container holding a sample containing microspheres (which may also include antibodies or antigens) is placed within the magnetic field of a permanent magnet. The spherical particles are drawn towards and collect on one side of the container to their magnetic properties, allowing for easy separation from non-magnetic components in the solution. However, both methods have limitations: EP-140787 requires manual handling which can be time consuming when processing multiple samples simultaneously; WO-86/06493 necessitates a strong permanent magnet that might not always provide optimal separation efficiency or could potentially damage sensitive biomaterials due to high magnetic field strength. texts.full_text: me you already know that part. (Let’s be honest—we’re not exactly an ‘unaccustomed to trailblazing’ bunch.) It’s those other times, the times when you really have no idea where to start, that the “do whatever you want” advice misses the point. Some examples of things you’re particularly expected to synthesize from the ether are: cake toppers, favors, and your vows. These things have traditional examples, but in modern times are prized for their one-of-a-kind nature that really represents you and your partner and no one else. They must be unmistakably you. Unfortunately, there’s no easy answer if you don’t know what that looks like. You just have to start poring through examples of each—tons of them—and start disqualifying the things you definitely don’t like. If there’s nothing left over that stands out, expand your search. Don’t just go looking for options, keep your target in mind so that when you’re out and about doing something completely different you’ll be open to that moment when the little light goes off in the back of your head—when something actually, unexpectedly seems like it could work. At the very least, know that you’re not alone. Everyone faces this ambivalence at some point in the planning process. Most of them figure it out in time, and the ones who don’t probably found that they didn’t need it at all. Unless it’s a fiancé, you need one of those. (Or not, what do I know! Just make it your own.) About the author Justin The naturally irreverent man-of-many-hats: musician, author, app developer, amateur photographer, and the creator of misters.com. Keep Promises: If you promise something to your child, make sure to follow through. Consistency in keeping promises helps build reliability and trust. 3. Listen Actively: Show that you value their thoughts and feelings by actively listening when they speak. This demonstrates respect and attentiveness. 4. Be Empathetic: Try to understand things from your child's perspective, even if it differs from yours. Displaying empathy shows them that you care about their emotions and experiences. 5.
